verses.text: ‘Whoso loveth instruction loveth knowledge.’ The Hebrew musar — discipline, correction — is the same word used throughout Proverbs for the formation that comes from accepting correction. Wisdom in Proverbs is not a possession; it is the result of being willing to be taught. The opposite — hating reproof — is named bluntly: brutish, like a beast that learns nothing.

Goodness draws God’s favour; wicked schemes draw His condemnation. The Hebrew ratson — favour, delight — names the warmth of God’s regard. Proverbs does not promise that good people will be rich or popular; it promises that their lives stand under God’s pleased look. The hidden economy of the universe is moral.

Wickedness builds nothing lasting; righteousness puts down roots. The Hebrew shoresh — root — evokes a plant whose stability lies underground. The wicked may flourish above the soil, but no anchor holds them. The righteous, however ordinary, are rooted. Psalm 1’s image of the tree by the water shares this picture.
12:4 Elsewhere in Proverbs a crown symbolizes wealth (14:24), long life (16:31), and grandchildren (17:6). • cancer (literally rot): A disgraceful wife is a deep and life-threatening problem.

12:7 The wicked might prosper momentarily, but they do not last. The family of the godly stands firm for generations.
12:8 To be sensible means to act only after reflecting on the consequences. A warped mind acts on impulse.
12:9 Reality is more important than appearance.
12:9 Reality is more important than appearance.
12:11 This proverb is repeated but with a different conclusion in 28:19.
12:12 The instability of thieves can produce violent behavior at any moment. By contrast, the godly are well rooted: Their relationships are stable even under duress.

12:15 Fools fail to realize what the wise know—that listening is the way to learn.
12:16 Keeping a level head allows a wise person to think clearly and avoid reactions that could cause a fight.
12:17 The message of this proverb is repeated in 14:5.
